system_description.overview: Kapwork is the AI platform for Receivables Finance. It issues tamper-proof digital certificates for receivables, transforming them into immediately fundable instruments, giving invoice owners direct capital access and giving asset-based lenders, structured finance teams, and invoice factors instant confidence in quality. Kapwork automates the verification of accounts receivable at the point of receipt.

products.Vendor Portal Agents: Automated sign-in to vendor management systems using vendor-provided credentials; AI-driven traversal of MFA and CAPTCHA challenges; scheduled extraction of invoice data with self-healing scrapers
products.Email Verification Agents: AI-powered ingestion and analysis of invoice-related emails and attachments; intelligent classification of invoice data fields across varied formats; continuous monitoring with human-in-the-loop notifications
products.Invoice Portfolio: Unified ledger combining data from both portals and emails in near-real time; comprehensive database of invoice lifecycle data; advanced analytics for portfolio insights, risk mitigation, anomaly detection, and dispute tracking
products.Ledger Integrations: Webhook capabilities to seamlessly push verified accounts receivable into external accounting and ERP systems

infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices or servers; all data processed on cloud services. HQ address: 24085 Summerhill Avenue, Los Altos, CA 94024

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Hosted in DigitalOcean VPC (default-sfo3 10.124.0.0/20 - SFO3). Segmented VPCs with access control lists (ACLs). Separate VPC in NYC1 (default-nyc1 10.116.0.0/20) for database replication.
network_architecture.firewalls: Two firewall profiles: 'kapwork-standard' (Inbound: 22 SSH, 80 HTTP, 443 HTTPS, 8080; Outbound: All TCP 22, 80, 443, 993, 10000, 25000, 59 UDP, 53 DNS) and 'eva' firewall. System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via DigitalOcean in conjunction with security policy network settings
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN)
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority for all web-based systems
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routing through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic enters through eva firewall (Inbound: 22 SSH, 80 HTTP, 443 HTTPS). Within VPC default-sfo3: dev-v.kapwork.com (10.124.0.3, kapwork-standard), prod-v.kapwork.com (10.124.0.3, kapwork-standard), email-agent-dev (10.124.0.15, eva), prod-email-agent.kapwork.com (10.124.0.X, eva). Primary PostgreSQL 17 database (d-postgres-db-prod-sfo3) in SFO3. Replication to read-only PostgreSQL 17 replica (db-ro-postgres-d-nyc1) in NYC1 VPC.

application_architecture.environments: At least 3: development (dev-v.kapwork.com), staging/test, production (prod-v.kapwork.com, prod-email-agent.kapwork.com). Changes tested in separate development/test environment before implementation (CC8.1.4).

data_storage.backup_frequency: Formal backup policies and procedures; suitable backups taken and maintained per Backup Policy. Specific frequency not disclosed.

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: DigitalOcean password policy / internal authentication system
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); two-factor authentication required for email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within 1 business day upon termination of employment
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through DigitalOcean Password policy; includes periodic forced changes, password expiry, and complexity requirements. User accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts. Guest/anonymous logins not allowed. Unattended desktops locked within time of inactivity.

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool with MFA access (specific tool not named)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Internal tool (specific tool not named); all changes documented from initiation through deployment and validation
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move changes to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Infrastructure as code - baseline configurations retained within the infrastructure as a code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly patch management reviews by CTO. Workstations configured to receive automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily (CC7.5.1).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not specifically named; monitoring tools used by IT team to continuously monitor system capacity and performance (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; monitoring tool generates alerts when predefined thresholds are met (CC5.2.1, CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure provider (data center services, managed databases, VPC, droplets)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers; auto-scan on continuous basis; definitions updated weekly for production servers; installed on Mac-based workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; frequency adjusted as required; performed by internal IT Infrastructure team (CC7.1.2)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party; results reviewed by IT management; high/critical findings remediated in timely manner (CC4.1.1)

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities: DigitalOcean is responsible for physical security of its data center; physical security controls not in scope

A1.2 - Environmental protections: Kapwork does not have an office; operates from a working space managed by building management; environmental security controls managed by building management / DigitalOcean
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2: Branch protection enforced - developers cannot make changes to production code without additional approval via merge request
CC8.1.3: Source code changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author; MFA required for source code tool access
CC6.1.1: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production
CC6.1.3: System components configured for per-tenant/customer access segmentation
CC7.4.1: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually; testing conducted per defined schedule for different loss scenarios
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
CC7.1.2: Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plans for critical/high vulnerabilities
CC9.2.1: Vendor management policy with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
CC6.6.1: Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured on application gateway and production network

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-region setup (SFO3 + NYC1) with database replication is good. However, DigitalOcean is a smaller cloud provider compared to AWS/Azure/GCP with more limited services. No CDN, no serverless, no advanced networking features mentioned. VPC segmentation is present but basic. The carve-out method means DigitalOcean's physical/environmental controls are excluded from audit scope.

application_architecture.rationale: No programming languages, frameworks, or specific tooling disclosed. The report mentions droplets (VMs) suggesting a traditional server-based deployment rather than modern containerized architecture. Branch protection and code review are positive. Multiple environments (dev/staging/prod) confirmed. However, the lack of specificity about the tech stack is concerning for due diligence.

data_layer.rationale: PostgreSQL 17 as primary database is a solid choice. Multi-region replication (SFO3 primary to NYC1 read-only replica) shows some resilience planning. Data classification and retention policies exist. Per-tenant segregation confirmed. However, no caching layer, no data warehouse, no file storage details, and backup frequency not specified.

security_posture.rationale: Good foundations: MFA for admin access, IDS/IPS, antivirus/malware protection, annual pen testing, monthly vuln scans, endpoint protection, server hardening. VPN/bastion host for remote access. However, no WAF mentioned, no SIEM named, no specific monitoring/APM tools named, no cybersecurity insurance. Encryption at rest uses cloud provider KMS but no specifics on algorithm/strength.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC mentioned (baseline configurations as code, configuration management tool with rollback). Branch protection with merge request approvals. Formal change management with emergency change process. Separate environments. However, no specific CI/CD tool named, no specific source control platform named, no container orchestration, no deployment pipeline details.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, documented, and reviewed annually. Testing conducted annually per defined schedule for different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ and multi-region failover capability (spinning up servers across all availability zones). Database replication to NYC1. However, RTO and RPO are not disclosed, and specific backup frequency is not stated.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavy single-vendor dependency on DigitalOcean for virtually all infrastructure: compute, networking, managed database, VPC, firewalls. No additional cloud providers, no CDN provider, no named monitoring/logging/SIEM vendors. Vendor management policy exists with annual reviews, but the actual vendor ecosystem is extremely concentrated.

overall.rationale: Kapwork demonstrates adequate security fundamentals for an early-stage company pursuing its first SOC 2 Type 1 audit. Multi-region database replication, MFA enforcement, branch protection, IaC, and annual pen testing are positives. However, significant gaps exist: heavy DigitalOcean dependency, no named monitoring/SIEM/CI-CD tools, no WAF, no disclosed RTO/RPO, no specific tech stack details, and limited vendor diversity. The report is notably light on technology specifics compared to more mature SOC 2 reports.

red_flags: Extreme single-vendor concentration on DigitalOcean for all infrastructure - creates significant platform risk
Metadata lists 'Render' as infrastructure provider but actual report describes DigitalOcean exclusively - discrepancy needs clarification
No WAF (Web Application Firewall) mentioned despite being a web-based SaaS platform handling sensitive financial data
No SIEM tool named despite handling financial receivables data
No specific APM/monitoring tool named despite claims of continuous monitoring
Processing Integrity trust criteria excluded despite the platform's core function being to verify and certify invoice data accuracy
Privacy trust criteria excluded despite handling business financial data
yellow_flags: SOC 2 Type 1 only (design adequacy, not operating effectiveness) - no observation period
No RTO or RPO disclosed despite having BCDR policy and testing
No programming languages or frameworks disclosed - limits ability to assess code quality risks
No specific CI/CD pipeline tool named (GitHub Actions, CircleCI, etc.)
No specific source control platform named (GitHub, GitLab, etc.)
No caching layer mentioned - could indicate performance/scalability limitations
No CDN mentioned for a web application
Backup frequency not specified despite having formal backup policy
Very small team (org chart shows ~8 people) - key-person risk and limited segregation of duties
TLS version not specified - could be using older versions
No container orchestration - running on bare VMs/droplets suggests less mature deployment
green_flags: Unqualified (clean) audit opinion with no exceptions noted
Multi-region database replication (SFO3 to NYC1) with PostgreSQL 17
MFA enforced for administrative production access, source code access, and email
Branch protection with mandatory merge request approvals before production deployment
Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
IaC approach with configuration management and rollback capability
Formal BCDR policy with annual testing across different loss scenarios
Quarterly user access reviews for all user accounts including admin
Access revoked within 1 business day upon termination
Formal vendor management with annual SOC report reviews
Server hardening against established entity standard with baseline configurations
Data classification and retention policies in place
Network diagram provided showing clear VPC segmentation with defined firewall rules
key_observations: The metadata file lists 'Render' as the infrastructure provider, but the SOC 2 report exclusively references DigitalOcean. This discrepancy could indicate a recent migration, an error in the metadata, or Render being used for non-production services outside audit scope.

For an AI platform handling financial receivables, the exclusion of Processing Integrity trust criteria is unusual and potentially concerning - this is the trust criteria most relevant to data accuracy in their core product.
The network diagram reveals a relatively simple architecture: 4 droplets in SFO3 (2 web servers, 2 email agent servers) plus databases. This is a small surface area suggesting an early-stage product.

The org chart shows approximately 8 people with Peter Thomas (CTO) as the signing authority. The team includes Full-Stack Software Engineers and a Financial Prompt Engineer - the latter title suggesting significant AI/LLM integration in the product.
Two distinct firewall profiles ('kapwork-standard' and 'eva') suggest different security postures for different service types - the web platform vs. the email agent component.

The report repeatedly reuses the same control descriptions across multiple trust criteria (e.g., CC1.4.1 and CC2.1.2 appear many times) which is common in template-driven SOC 2 reports from smaller audit firms and may indicate less depth in the actual control testing.
PostgreSQL 17 is the very latest version (released late 2024), suggesting the team keeps their database stack current.

The email agent servers have a different firewall profile ('eva') than the main web servers ('kapwork-standard'), with different inbound rules including port 993 (IMAPS) which aligns with the email ingestion functionality.
